item.title: The Grand Canal of Venice (Blue Venice)
item.lesson: Daylight returns, but it does not bring the old certainty back. Manet spent a month in Venice in 1874 — the very autumn his younger friends in Paris were being mocked under a new insult, 'Impressionists' — and painted the Grand Canal as no veduta painter would have dared: cropped tight, casually off-center, the water taking up half the picture and the famous domes of the Salute reduced to a supporting role in the top corner. The real subject is blue. Water-blue, sky-blue, the blue-striped mooring poles the Venetians call pali, all of it laid on in frank, separate strokes that refuse to melt into illusion. Stand close and it is paint; step back and it is noon. Manet reportedly declared the picture finished when the light itself changed. That is the modern eye's confession: the painting was never of Venice, exactly. It was of one hour of Venice — and the hour, not the city, is what he signed.
item.lookFor: Follow the twisting blue-and-white mooring poles — the most solid objects in the picture, and the only ones painted with the crispness Canaletto once spent on cathedrals.
